description:
You are acting as a database-reliability engineer who has just received an
    incomplete backup configuration for a new PostgreSQL cluster.  
    Two configuration files already exist on disk:

        1. /home/user/db_backup/config.yaml
        2. /home/user/db_backup/settings.toml

    Your job is to **edit both files in-place** so that they contain the exact
    values listed below, then create a short human-readable log file that
    records what you did.  No additional files, extensions or directories
    may be introduced.

    ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    1. YAML FILE – /home/user/db_backup/config.yaml
    ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    After you have edited it, the file **must contain exactly** the following
    eight lines (eight newline characters, UNIX line-endings).  Do **not**
    introduce extra spaces before or after any key, value or colon and keep
    the indentation exactly as shown:

        version: "2.1"
        retention_days: 30
        compression: "lz4"
        backup_type: "incremental"
        schedules:
          nightly: "02:00"
          weekly: "Sunday 03:00"
        encryption: "aes256"

    Important notes:
      • retention_days is changed from 7 → 30  
      • backup_type is changed from “full” → “incremental”  
      • Two new schedule keys (nightly & weekly) must be added and indented
        exactly two spaces under “schedules:”.  
      • Do **not** sort or reorder the keys; keep the exact sequence shown
        above.

    ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    2. TOML FILE – /home/user/db_backup/settings.toml
    ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    After your edits, this file must contain **exactly** the block below,
    including comments, blank lines and ordering.  Preserve the square-bracket
    headers and indentation precisely.

        # Application-level settings
        [app]
        name = "pg-backup-agent"
        version = "1.3.0"

        # Storage backend
        [storage]
        provider = "s3"
        region   = "us-west-2"
        bucket   = "prod-backups"

        # Databases being backed up
        [[databases]]
        name     = "production"
        host     = "prod.db.internal"
        port     = 5432

        [[databases]]
        name     = "analytics"
        host     = "analytics.db.internal"
        port     = 5432

    Key points:
      • app.version must be bumped from 1.2.3 → 1.3.0  
      • The [storage] table is completely overwritten with the
        provider/region/bucket values shown.  
      • Exactly two [[databases]] tables must exist—in the order “production”
        then “analytics”—each with the three keys shown.

    ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    3. LOG FILE – /home/user/db_backup/operation_summary.log
    ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    When you are finished editing both configuration files, create a new log
    file named “operation_summary.log” **in the same directory**.  It must
    contain **exactly three lines**, each following this template:

        TIMESTAMP_PLACEHOLDER <free-form message>

    For this exercise, do **not** generate a real timestamp; literally write
    the string “TIMESTAMP_PLACEHOLDER”, followed by a single space, followed
    by the messages shown below.  The three required lines, in order, are:

        TIMESTAMP_PLACEHOLDER config.yaml updated successfully
        TIMESTAMP_PLACEHOLDER settings.toml updated successfully
        TIMESTAMP_PLACEHOLDER Backup configuration changes complete

    The automated grader will verify:
      • The log file exists at the correct path.
      • It has exactly three lines (three newline characters).
      • Each line begins with the literal text “TIMESTAMP_PLACEHOLDER ” and
        then the exact message (case-sensitive).

    ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    Summary of what you must deliver
    ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      • /home/user/db_backup/config.yaml — eight lines, content exactly as
        specified.
      • /home/user/db_backup/settings.toml — content exactly as specified.
      • /home/user/db_backup/operation_summary.log — three-line file in the
        format described above.

    All paths are under /home/user, so you do not need elevated privileges.
    Once everything is in place, exit—no further output is required.
truth:
<!-- The values below are the canonical answers used by the test suite. -->
    <config_yaml_content>
version: "2.1"
retention_days: 30
compression: "lz4"
backup_type: "incremental"
schedules:
  nightly: "02:00"
  weekly: "Sunday 03:00"
encryption: "aes256"
    </config_yaml_content>

    <settings_toml_content>
# Application-level settings
[app]
name = "pg-backup-agent"
version = "1.3.0"

# Storage backend
[storage]
provider = "s3"
region   = "us-west-2"
bucket   = "prod-backups"

# Databases being backed up
[[databases]]
name     = "production"
host     = "prod.db.internal"
port     = 5432

[[databases]]
name     = "analytics"
host     = "analytics.db.internal"
port     = 5432
    </settings_toml_content>

    <operation_summary_log>
TIMESTAMP_PLACEHOLDER config.yaml updated successfully
TIMESTAMP_PLACEHOLDER settings.toml updated successfully
TIMESTAMP_PLACEHOLDER Backup configuration changes complete
    </operation_summary_log>
